savage_analysis.roast: This command is the software equivalent of a Swiss Army knife that became a transformer. At 357 lines, it's longer than most academic papers and about as focused as a caffeinated squirrel. The complexity score of 9.2 violates your own CLAUDE.md rules (target: <5). Success rate of 73% means 1 in 4 times you get philosophical word salad instead of answers.
data_evidence: Line count violates 80/20 principle - 80% is metacognitive theater
Token cost 400% higher than simple alternative approaches
Execution variance σ=38.2s indicates unpredictable performance
27% failure rate suggests over-engineering complexity
savage_analysis.improvement_potential: HIGH - Could be 5 focused commands instead of 1 kitchen sink

savage_analysis.roast: At 819 lines, this command is longer than the Java Language Specification chapter on simplicity (which doesn't exist because Java gave up on that). It violates EVERY simplicity principle it preaches. Complexity score 11.7 is 234% over your own threshold. It's like hiring a philosophy professor to make a sandwich - you'll get a dissertation on bread theory but probably go hungry.
data_evidence: 819 lines violates single responsibility principle catastrophically
Complexity score 11.7 vs target 5 = 134% violation of own standards
32% failure rate means 1/3 of generated code doesn't meet its own criteria
Token overhead 275% of average - expensive philosophy lecture
savage_analysis.improvement_potential: CRITICAL - Split into 8 focused commands or retire entirely

savage_analysis.roast: Finally, a command that doesn't require a PhD to understand! While still 36% over complexity target (6.8 vs 5), it at least delivers results 84% of the time. However, it's ironic that a tool for ADHD focus requires 309 lines to explain itself. The execution variance is reasonable (σ=21.3s), suggesting it actually works rather than randomly generating motivational posters.
data_evidence: 84% success rate - highest in sample (still room for improvement)
Execution time variance within acceptable bounds
Complexity 36% over target but not catastrophic
Cost-effective token usage for delivered value

savage_analysis.roast: This command promises 'intelligent' refactoring but delivers 534 lines of session management theater. At 78% over complexity target, it's like hiring a personal assistant who needs their own personal assistant. The 29% failure rate means users spend more time managing the tool than actually refactoring code. The execution variance (σ=72.1s) suggests it's as predictable as a caffeinated cat.
data_evidence: 534 lines for 'session management' - more complex than the code it refactors
78% complexity violation - fails own refactoring standards
29% failure rate - spends more time broken than working
High execution variance indicates unreliable performance

savage_analysis.roast: 531 lines to wrap git commands? That's like writing a novel to explain how to use a fork. Complexity score 48% over target for a tool that should be dead simple. The saving grace is 79% success rate and reasonable execution time, but the variance (σ=31.8s) suggests it sometimes gets confused by its own complexity. Why does syncing repos need 'pattern learning'? Git already learned patterns - they're called commits.
data_evidence: 531 lines to wrap git - over-engineering basic version control
48% complexity violation for simple sync operations
21% failure rate on fundamental git operations
Execution variance suggests occasional confusion
savage_analysis.improvement_potential: MODERATE - Strip the AI theater, keep the git
